topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammering started early, a rhythmic, insistent intrusion into my morning quiet. I peered through the blinds, a slow, deliberate movement, my jaw tightening as I watched Mr. Henderson wrestling with a length of wire. He was a man of grand pronouncements and sudden, impulsive projects, and this felt like one of his. I leaned against the doorframe, arms crossed tightly over my chest, observing the process with a detached air. *Another thing to change,* I thought, a familiar knot forming in my stomach. I made a show of adjusting my reading glasses, pretending deep interest in the texture of the wallpaper, while cataloging every unnecessary spike and post he drove into my shared property line.

[story 2]

"Just a quick thing, Carol," Mr. Henderson called out from his yard, his voice carrying that irritatingly cheerful timbre. I was watering my petunias, my watering can held steady, but my gaze drifted toward the construction site. I stopped mid-pour, letting the water run down my fingers. I watched him measure the distance between two oak trees, his brow furrowed in concentration. I took a slow, deep breath, the air feeling suddenly thin. *This is going to take up a lot of space,* I mused internally. When he finally looked over, expecting approval, I offered a tight, practiced smile that didn't quite reach my eyes, and pointed vaguely toward the overgrown hedge.

[story 3]

I found myself pacing the length of my living room floor, the carpet fibers feeling oddly coarse beneath my bare feet. The construction noise was relentless now, a grating soundtrack to my growing unease. I kept checking my phone, half-expecting an official notice or perhaps a zoning violation. Mr. Henderson was standing near the property line, gesturing emphatically with a tape measure. His enthusiasm was palpable, almost overwhelming, and it made my own posture defensive. I crossed my arms over my chest again, a subtle but firm barrier. "It’s certainly... a statement," I managed to say, my voice coming out flatter than intended, as I watched him secure a post with a heavy, decisive swing of the hammer.
